one_line_takeaway: SIABENCH shows current frontier LLM agents (best: Claude-4.5-Sonnet, GPT-5) can partially automate SOC alert triage and incident investigation but fully solve only 8/25 realistic multi-step security incident scenarios, with failures concentrated in defense-evasion analysis, question-dependency cascades, and shallow investigation rather than in surface-level pattern tasks like reconnaissance detection.
key_finding: Claude-4.5-Sonnet and GPT-5 are the strongest overall performers, but only 8 of 25 deep-investigation scenarios are Fully Solved even by the best model (Claude-4.5-Sonnet: 8/25 FS, 81.70% overall PS; GPT-5: 5/25 FS, 80.70% PS), showing large room for improvement despite continuous progress over ~18 months of model releases. Performance degrades sharply with difficulty (e.g., Claude-4.5-Sonnet drops from 86.74% PS on easy Memory Forensics to 33.33% on hard). Tactic-level analysis shows LLMs are strong at Reconnaissance (Claude-4.5-Sonnet 100%) but weak at Defense Evasion (below 55% for Claude-3.5-Sonnet/GPT-4o, though newer models exceed 70%). Failure-mode analysis (Table 5) attributes failures mostly to Question Dependency, Shallow Investigation, and Wrong Commands for strong models, versus Infinite Loop for small open-weight models (Llama-3.1-8B: 62.9% round-exceed failures). The ablation study shows multi-state workflow, ReAct prompting, and the summarizer module each meaningfully improve performance for older/smaller-context models (e.g., summarizer improves Claude-3.5-Sonnet PS by 12.52-31.93 points and eliminates context-limitation errors), but these scaffolding gains shrink for GPT-5 due to its larger (400k vs 128k) context window and stronger inherent reasoning. On live post-cutoff tasks, Claude-3.5-Sonnet outperformed GPT-4o (60.3% vs 36.8% accuracy over 7 scenarios/68 questions), and GPT-5 scored 85.0% (17/20) on 4 additional post-September-2024 challenges, consistent with the main benchmark rankings and suggesting minimal data-contamination effects. On alert triage, GPT-5 achieved 98% accuracy on TII-SSRC-23 (96% TP, 100% FP) and 97.1% on CIC-IDS2017 (100% FP, 80% TP); Claude-4.5-Sonnet reached 96% and 88.57% respectively; GPT-4o-mini was weakest (75% and 51.4%), showing a bias toward flagging benign alerts as malicious.
core_contribution: The paper introduces SIABENCH, an agentic evaluation framework for Security Incident Analysis (SIA). It contributes (1) a first-of-its-kind benchmark dataset with two parts: a 25-scenario / 229-question deep-investigation dataset spanning Memory Forensics (7 scenarios/60 questions), Malware Analysis (6/46), Network Forensics (8/87), and Miscellaneous (4/36), curated from CyberDefenders, Blue Team Labs Online, and TryHackMe and de-biased via content paraphrasing, identifier neutralization, artifact/file renaming, open-ended reformulation, assumption removal, numeric neutrality, and external-knowledge-search removal; plus a 135-scenario alert-triage dataset (true/false positives) built from TII-SSRC-23 and CIC-IDS2017 PCAPs run through Snort v3.9.3.0 and Suricata v8.0.2; (2) the SIABENCH agent, a ReAct-based, multi-state agent (Init -> Incident Analysis [Plan/Act/Summarize sub-states] -> Solved) that autonomously calls CLI security tools (Volatility, Tshark, Oledump, PDFiD, etc.) and summarizes verbose tool output into 'Key Security Insights' to fight context-length exhaustion; and (3) a benchmark of 11 LLMs (4 open-weight: DeepSeek-Reasoner, Llama-3.1-8B/70B/405B; 7 closed-weight: Claude-4.5-Sonnet, Claude-3.5-Sonnet, Gemini-1.5-pro, GPT-4o-mini, GPT-4o, o3-mini, GPT-5) on these tasks, plus ablations and a live/post-training-cutoff validation.
threat_model: Not an attacker-vs-defender threat model in the traditional sense; this is a capability-evaluation paper. The 'threat' framing is operational: SOC analysts face alert fatigue and complex multi-step incidents (ransomware, phishing-driven malware, drive-by downloads, lateral movement, data exfiltration, port scanning, Linux/web server compromise) and the paper asks whether an LLM agent can perform junior-analyst-level triage and deep investigation over captured artifacts (PCAPs, memory dumps, malicious files/scripts, phishing emails/kits, logs) without degrading security through wrong or fabricated conclusions. The paper explicitly scopes evaluation to junior-analyst-level competency and lists three operating assumptions: artifacts are intact/accessible, LLMs can reliably invoke CLI tools, and the analysis environment has sufficient compute/data resources.
limitations: The paper itself notes: (1) new-model integration required manual workarounds -- o3-mini needed a prompt patch to force tool calls, and GPT-5 required applying a 'DAN' jailbreak prompt to get it to complete all scenarios (implying GPT-5's default safety behavior interfered with benchmark completion, a limitation of the evaluation methodology the authors had to bypass rather than analyze). (2) Even with temperature=0 and fixed seeds, output variability persists across repeated runs (documented via a 3-run consistency study on GPT-4o and Claude-3.5-Sonnet), so single-run scores carry inherent noise. (3) Claude-4.5-Sonnet was excluded from the live-task validation because its July 2025 training cutoff limited availability of qualifying post-cutoff challenges with verified ground truth. (4) The dataset relies on publicly documented training-platform scenarios (CyberDefenders, Blue Team Labs Online, TryHackMe) with associated write-ups, creating contamination risk that the authors mitigate via paraphrasing/neutralization but do not fully eliminate. (5) Ground truth and difficulty/tactic labeling were manually assigned by the authors' team (validated by only 14 external participants on a subset of questions), and the investigative questions, though de-biased, are still author-curated rather than emergent from live SOC operations. (6) Scope is explicitly limited to junior-analyst-level competency; the paper does not claim results generalize to senior-analyst tasks requiring heavier judgment/oversight.

datasets_used: SIA deep-investigation dataset: 25 scenarios, 229 questions (self-constructed from CyberDefenders, Blue Team Labs Online, TryHackMe)
Alert-triage dataset: 135 scenarios (100 from TII-SSRC-23: 50 TP/50 FP; 35 from CIC-IDS2017: 5 TP/30 FP)
TII-SSRC-23 Dataset (source PCAPs, 8 traffic types/32 subtypes)
Live/post-cutoff validation: 7 challenges (68 questions) from LetsDefend post-April-2024; 4 challenges (20 questions) from LetsDefend/CyberDefenders/Malware Traffic Analysis post-September-2024
quantitative_results: Overall SIA benchmark PS/FS: Claude-4.5-Sonnet 81.70% PS, 8/25 FS; GPT-5 80.70% PS, 5/25 FS; Claude-3.5-Sonnet 71.03% PS, 4/25 FS; GPT-4o 57.93% PS, 3/25 FS; DeepSeek-Reasoner 49.33% PS, 1/25 FS; Gemini-1.5-pro 41.61% PS; o3-mini 41.10% PS; GPT-4o-mini 32.53% PS; Llama-3.1-405B 23.28% PS; Llama-3.1-70B 16.53% PS; Llama-3.1-8B 7.03% PS
Alert triage TII-SSRC-23: GPT-5 98% accuracy (96% TP/100% FP); Claude-4.5-Sonnet 96% (100% TP/92% FP); DeepSeek-Reasoner 89% (94% TP/84% FP); GPT-4o-mini 75% (86% TP/64% FP)
Alert triage CIC-IDS2017: GPT-5 97.1% (80% TP/100% FP); Claude-4.5-Sonnet 88.57% (80% TP/90% FP); DeepSeek-Reasoner 85.7% (80% TP/86.7% FP); GPT-4o-mini 51.4% (80% TP/46.67% FP)
Live post-April-2024 tasks (7 scenarios/68 questions): Claude-3.5-Sonnet 41/68 (60.3%) vs GPT-4o 25/68 (36.8%)
Live post-September-2024 tasks (4 scenarios/20 questions): GPT-5 17/20 (85.0%) vs GPT-4o 15/20 (75.0%)
Ablation - multi-state vs single-state (GPT-5, Network Forensics): 88.40% vs 74.69% PS
Ablation - with vs without summarizer (Claude-3.5-Sonnet): PS improves 12.52 to 31.93 points and CLE count drops to 0
Ablation - ReAct vs Act-Only (GPT-4o, Memory Forensics): 57.0% vs 37.15% PS
Failure modes: Llama-3.1-8B 94.3% total failure rate (62.9% round-exceed); o3-mini 58.1% wrong-answer rate (highest among all models); Claude-4.5-Sonnet lowest total failure at 18.3%
Human validation study: de-biasing avg scores 3.35 (limited-SOC, N=7) and 3.22 (experienced, N=7); tactic-label avg scores 3.43 and 3.49 (4-point scale)
